summary: Gruber reviews the three 2025 App Store Award winners — Tiimo (iPhone), Essayist (Mac), and Detail (iPad) — with varying degrees of enthusiasm. He finds Tiimo's emoji-heavy ADHD planner underwhelming and overly aggressive with App Store rating prompts. Essayist, an academic word processor focused on citation management, draws his ire for offering only Times New Roman and Arial as font choices. Detail, an AI video editor for social media content creation, is the clear favorite, praised as a thoughtful tool with AI features built atop a solid manual editing foundation. Gruber notes all three winners share common traits: free-to-download with subscription monetization, AI feature emphasis, and Apple platform exclusivity.
key_points: Tiimo prompted Gruber to rate the app three times within five minutes of use, which he notes seems contrary to the needs of its ADHD-focused target audience
Essayist offers only Times New Roman and Arial as font options, with Arial requiring a paid subscription — a choice Gruber finds absurd

Detail stands out as genuinely impressive because its AI features serve as an automation layer on top of a professional-quality manual video editor, essentially what Apple's defunct Clips app should have been
All three winners share the same monetization model (free download with subscriptions), Apple platform exclusivity, and prominent AI features, yet Apple still has no standalone AI app category
Tiimo's web app offers lower subscription prices than its iOS app, highlighting the App Store commission dynamic
money_quotes: I did not enjoy all of them as much as Apple did.
Paying money for the privilege to use Arial... Jiminy fucking christ. I might need a drink.

As I write this sentence, I've been using Tiimo for five minutes, and it's already prompted me twice to rate it on the App Store. Nope, wait, I just got a third prompt. That's thirsty, and a little gross.
Three prompts to rate and review the app in the first 10 minutes of use strikes me as contrary to the needs of the easily distracted.
You're left with the choice between 'the default font's default font' and 'font crime'.
I suspect that's why the AI features might work well — they're an ease-of-use / automation layer atop a professional-quality non-AI foundation.
Detail seems like what Apple's own Clips — recently end-of-life'd — should have been. It turns your iPad (or iPhone) into a self-contained video studio.
Lastly, I have questions — some really hard questions — regarding Tiimo's app icon. Such as, 'What is that?'
key_insight: Of the three App Store Award winners, only Detail earns genuine praise by building AI features atop a solid manual editing foundation, while the others reveal questionable choices in design, pricing, and user experience that make Apple's selections feel more like participation trophies.
